relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Ephraim's wounded honor rises against Gideon.
Gideon answers by making their contribution larger than his own.
Shared honor cools their anger.
The three hundred cross Jordan exhausted but still pursuing.
Gideon asks Succoth for bread for weary men.
Succoth refuses to risk support before victory is secure.
Gideon's answer turns hunger into a threat of torture.
Penuel refuses in the same way.
Gideon threatens its tower after he returns.
The surviving eastern army waits at Karkor, diminished from the fallen host.
Gideon attacks while the camp feels secure.
Zebah and Zalmunna flee and are captured.
Gideon returns by Heres with vengeance still unfinished.
A young man is seized and made to identify Succoth's leaders.
Gideon confronts Succoth with the kings they doubted he would capture.
He teaches Succoth through thorns and briers—the ugly schooling of revenge.
Penuel's tower falls, and men of the city die.
Gideon asks the kings about brothers killed at Tabor.
Family blood turns the capture into personal execution.
He tells his firstborn to kill, but the youth is afraid.
Gideon kills the kings himself and takes their ornaments.
Israel asks Gideon to found a ruling line.
He refuses the title and says YHWH will rule.
Yet he asks for gold from the spoils.
The people willingly fill the garment with earrings.
Gold, crescents, pendants, purple, and camel chains gather around the deliverer.
The ephod becomes a snare in Ophrah.
Midian lowers its head, and the land rests forty years.
Jerubbaal returns to his house.
Seventy sons reveal the scale of his household power.
A Shechem concubine bears Abimelech, whose name whispers kingship.
Gideon dies and is buried with his father.
After him, Israel turns back to the Baals and Baal-berith.
The rescuer is forgotten because YHWH is forgotten.
The house of Gideon receives no enduring covenant loyalty.

contemplative_note: Judges 8 shows how a deliverer can curdle after victory. Gideon cools Ephraim with humility, then tortures Succoth, kills Penuel, gathers gold, refuses kingship in words while building king-like power in practice. The chapter is a warning about what unhealed victory can become.
